methodology: Cross-referenced CalorieKing, FatSecret, USDA FoodData Central (rice noodles #20133, beef broth #06170, fish sauce #06179), MyFitnessPal community averages, and EatThisMuch. Vietnamese restaurants rarely publish nutrition facts, so all values are ESTIMATES based on typical recipes, ingredient-level USDA data, and portion norms. Pho component breakdown derived from USDA rice noodles (109 cal/cup cooked), beef broth (17 cal/cup), and standard protein portions. Banh mi based on standard 12-inch baguette (~250 cal bread) + fillings. Rice plate portions assume 1.5 cups cooked rice (~310 cal) + protein + garnishes.

items.notes: Pure beef bone broth without noodles or protein. Simmered 12-24 hours with star anise, cinnamon, ginger, fish sauce. Extremely low calorie — the 'free' part of pho. USDA beef broth #06170. Most bowls contain 2-3 cups of broth (80-150 cal total).
items.lean_version: Already the leanest component. Ask for extra broth instead of extra noodles for volume with minimal calories.

items.notes: Chicken bone broth base for Pho Ga. Slightly lighter than beef broth. Same aromatics (star anise, cinnamon, ginger). USDA chicken broth #06172.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest component.

items.notes: Flat rice noodles (banh pho). THIS is where the calories hide in pho. A small bowl has ~1 cup noodles (192 cal), large bowl has 1.5-2 cups (290-384 cal). USDA rice noodles #20133. Pure carbs, almost zero protein. The biggest lever for making pho lean.
items.lean_version: Ask for HALF noodles or noodle-less pho (just broth + protein + herbs). Saves 100-200 cal instantly. Some places offer zucchini noodle substitution.

items.notes: Small bowl: ~1 cup noodles + 3 oz rare beef slices + 1.5 cups broth + herbs. Rare beef (tai) is thinly sliced eye round that cooks in hot broth. Breakdown: broth 60 cal, noodles 192 cal, beef 130 cal. One of the leaner pho options. FatSecret generic pho with beef: 176 cal/cup.
items.lean_version: Half noodles: ~280 cal. No noodles (broth + beef + herbs): ~190 cal. Add extra bean sprouts and Thai basil for volume.

items.notes: Large bowl: ~2 cups noodles + 5 oz rare beef + 2.5 cups broth + herbs. Breakdown: broth 100 cal, noodles 384 cal, beef 215 cal. The noodle portion is the biggest calorie jump from small to large (+192 cal).
items.lean_version: Order large but ask for small noodle portion — get the extra broth and protein without the carb jump. ~420 cal.

items.notes: The works: rare beef, brisket, meatball, tripe, tendon in large bowl. Brisket adds fat (+40 cal vs tai), meatballs add fat/carbs. Tripe and tendon are actually very lean (tendon is mostly collagen, ~70 cal/3oz). The fattiest pho option due to brisket.
items.lean_version: Ask for tai + tendon only (skip brisket and meatball). Tendon is mostly collagen protein with almost zero fat. Saves ~60 cal.

items.notes: Chicken broth base with poached chicken (usually thigh or breast mix), rice noodles, herbs. Lighter broth than beef pho. Chicken is leaner than brisket/meatball combo. The lightest standard protein pho. Breakdown: broth 70 cal, noodles 290 cal, chicken 120 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for white meat only (breast). Half noodles drops to ~340 cal. Extra lime and herbs add zero calories.

items.notes: Vegetable broth with rice noodles, tofu, bok choy, mushrooms, bean sprouts, herbs. Lowest calorie pho but also lowest protein — you'll be hungry in 2 hours. Add tofu or request a side of chicken to fix the protein gap.
items.lean_version: Already lean on calories. The issue is LOW PROTEIN. Add a side of chicken breast (+140 cal, +26g protein) to make it satiating.

items.notes: Crusty baguette (~250 cal) + pork cold cuts + liver pate + mayo + pickled daikon/carrot + cilantro + jalapeno. The pate and mayo are the fat bombs (~15g fat combined). Sodexo Banh Mi: 410 cal. Mendocino Farms pork belly version: 780 cal. Average street-style is 480-560 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for no pate, light mayo (or skip mayo). Saves 100-150 cal. The bread is unavoidable but the pickled veggies are essentially free calories.

items.notes: Baguette + grilled lemongrass chicken thigh + pickled veggies + cilantro + mayo. Leaner than classic pork because no pate. Chicken thigh adds some fat vs breast. Good protein-to-calorie ratio for a sandwich.
items.lean_version: Request chicken breast instead of thigh, skip mayo. ~390 cal. Best banh mi option for macros.

items.notes: Baguette + grilled pork (often caramelized with lemongrass/sugar) + pickled veggies + mayo. The caramelized sugar glaze on pork adds sugar. Fattier than chicken but very flavorful. FatSecret pork banh mi: 632 cal (high-end restaurant portion).
items.lean_version: Skip mayo and pate if included. ~440 cal. Can't avoid the caramelized glaze — that's what makes it taste good.

items.notes: Baguette + fried or grilled tofu + pickled veggies + cilantro + sriracha mayo. Lowest calorie banh mi but also lowest protein. Tofu is often fried, adding fat. Without mayo: ~360 cal.
items.lean_version: Ask for grilled (not fried) tofu, skip mayo. ~350 cal. Low protein though — pair with edamame or a protein drink.

items.notes: Baguette + lemongrass-marinated grilled chicken + pickled veggies + herbs + mayo. Similar to grilled chicken banh mi but lemongrass marinade may include sugar and oil. Best protein-to-calorie ratio of all banh mi options.
items.lean_version: Skip mayo, request extra pickled veggies. ~410 cal with 32g protein. The best banh mi for macros.

items.dish_name: Com Tam — Broken Rice + Grilled Pork Chop
items.notes: 1.5 cups broken rice (~310 cal) + grilled bone-in pork chop (~300 cal with fat/marinade) + fried egg (~90 cal) + fish sauce + pickled veggies + cucumber. The rice and pork chop fat are the calorie drivers. Often comes with a side of fish sauce dressing (nuoc cham) adding 20-30 cal.
items.lean_version: Half rice (~155 cal saved), skip fried egg (~90 cal saved), trim visible fat from pork chop. ~535 cal. Ask for extra cucumber/pickled veggies.

items.dish_name: Com Ga Nuong — Grilled Chicken Over Rice
items.notes: 1.5 cups white rice (~310 cal) + grilled chicken thigh/leg (~250 cal with marinade) + pickled veggies + fish sauce. Leaner than pork chop plate. Chicken is usually thigh (more fat) marinated in lemongrass/garlic/sugar. Still a big plate because of rice volume.
items.lean_version: Half rice, request chicken breast if available. ~480 cal with 40g+ protein. One of the best Vietnamese restaurant orders for macros.

items.dish_name: Bo Luc Lac — Shaking Beef Over Rice
items.notes: Cubed beef tenderloin/sirloin wok-seared with garlic, soy sauce, butter, served over rice with watercress salad and lime-pepper dipping sauce. The butter and oil in the wok add significant fat. FatSecret shaking beef (without rice): 260 cal, 38g protein. Rice adds ~310 cal.
items.lean_version: Half rice, ask for light oil/butter. The beef itself is lean (tenderloin). Without rice on a bed of watercress: ~360 cal, 38g protein. EXCELLENT lean option without rice.

items.dish_name: Bo Luc Lac — Shaking Beef (No Rice)
items.notes: Just the shaking beef over watercress with lime-pepper dip, no rice. 38g protein for 310 cal is ELITE. The butter/oil in cooking adds fat but the beef is tender and high quality. One of the best protein-per-calorie Vietnamese dishes.
items.lean_version: Ask for light oil/butter in the wok. ~260 cal with 38g protein. Absolute best Vietnamese restaurant order for cutting.

items.dish_name: Bun Thit Nuong — Grilled Pork Vermicelli
items.notes: Vermicelli rice noodles (~250 cal) + grilled pork (~200 cal) + lettuce, bean sprouts, pickled carrots, herbs + nuoc cham fish sauce dressing (~40 cal). Served cold/room temp. The nuoc cham dressing has sugar. Pork is often marinated with sugar and fish sauce for caramelization.
items.lean_version: Half noodles, extra lettuce/herbs. ~490 cal. The fresh herbs and vegetables are essentially free — load up.

items.dish_name: Bun Cha — Grilled Pork Patties + Noodles
items.notes: Hanoi-style: grilled pork patties + pork belly slices in sweet fish sauce broth, served with vermicelli noodles and fresh herbs on the side. The dipping broth is sweetened (fish sauce + sugar + vinegar). Fattier than bun thit nuong because of pork belly component. This is the dish Obama ate in Hanoi.
items.lean_version: Eat the patties, skip the pork belly slices. Use less dipping broth (the sugar adds up). Half noodles. ~450 cal.

items.dish_name: Bun Bo Hue — Spicy Beef Noodle Soup
items.notes: Central Vietnamese spicy soup: thick round rice noodles + beef shank + pork knuckle + lemongrass-chili broth. Broth is richer/spicier than regular pho (lemongrass, shrimp paste, chili oil). Uses thicker noodles than pho (more cal per cup). Fattier cuts of meat (shank, knuckle). FatSecret pho bo: 176 cal/cup baseline.
items.lean_version: Ask for lean beef cuts only (skip pork knuckle), half noodles. ~420 cal. The spicy broth is low cal — enjoy the heat.

items.dish_name: Bun Cha Gio — Vermicelli with Fried Spring Rolls
items.notes: Vermicelli noodles + 3-4 fried egg rolls + lettuce, herbs, bean sprouts + nuoc cham. The fried spring rolls are the calorie bomb (~150-200 cal each). Higher fat than other bun dishes because of the deep-fried rolls. Often also includes grilled meat.
items.lean_version: Swap fried rolls for fresh spring rolls (goi cuon). Saves 200+ cal. Or ask for 2 rolls instead of 4.

items.dish_name: Goi Cuon — Fresh Spring Rolls (Shrimp + Pork)
items.notes: Rice paper wrapper + shrimp + thin pork slice + vermicelli + lettuce + herbs (mint, Thai basil). Only ~80 cal per roll — one of the LEANEST Vietnamese items. The rice paper is almost calorie-free. Served with peanut dipping sauce (add 45 cal/tbsp). FatSecret shrimp spring roll: 67 cal/roll.
items.lean_version: Already very lean. Watch the peanut sauce — that's where the calories hide. Use fish sauce (nuoc mam) as dip instead: ~6 cal/tbsp vs 45 cal/tbsp for peanut sauce.

items.dish_name: Goi Cuon — Fresh Spring Rolls (Shrimp Only)
items.notes: Same as above but without the pork slice. Slightly fewer calories and less fat. Shrimp is very lean (~7 cal per shrimp). Great starter that won't wreck your meal budget.
items.lean_version: Already lean. Skip or limit peanut sauce. Dip in soy sauce or nuoc mam instead.

items.notes: Rice paper + tofu + vermicelli + lettuce + herbs. Slightly more fat than shrimp version if tofu is fried. Lower protein than shrimp/pork. FatSecret vegetable spring roll: 63 cal/roll.
items.lean_version: Request grilled tofu not fried. ~130 cal for 2 rolls.

items.notes: Deep-fried rolls: ground pork, shrimp, glass noodles, mushroom, carrot in rice paper wrapper. ~170 cal per roll. DOUBLE the calories of fresh spring rolls. The deep frying adds 100+ cal per roll in oil. Crispy and delicious but a calorie trap. FatSecret pork spring roll: 74 cal/roll (NOT fried — fried versions are 150-200 cal each).
items.lean_version: Swap for fresh spring rolls (goi cuon): 80 cal/roll vs 170 cal/roll. Same fillings, half the calories. If you MUST have fried, eat 1 not 3.

items.notes: Single fried egg roll for easier tracking. Most appetizer orders come with 3-4 rolls (510-680 cal total). Know the per-unit cost so you can budget. Panda Express vegetable spring roll: 80 cal (smaller, less filling).
items.lean_version: One fried roll + fresh spring rolls for the rest. Or skip entirely.

items.dish_name: Ca Phe Sua Da — Vietnamese Iced Coffee
items.notes: Dark roast Vietnamese coffee brewed through a phin filter + 2-3 tbsp sweetened condensed milk + ice. The coffee itself is ~5 cal — ALL the calories come from sweetened condensed milk (130 cal/2 tbsp). 7 Leaves Vietnamese Coffee: 210 cal. Larger sizes can hit 290-430 cal. 28g sugar = 7 teaspoons. A hidden meal's worth of sugar.
items.lean_version: Ask for HALF sweetened condensed milk: ~120 cal. Or ask for black coffee with a splash of regular milk: ~20 cal. Going from ca phe sua da to black Vietnamese coffee saves 200 cal every visit.

items.dish_name: Ca Phe Den Da — Vietnamese Black Iced Coffee
items.notes: Same strong Vietnamese phin-drip coffee but WITHOUT sweetened condensed milk, just over ice. Essentially zero calories. The swap from ca phe sua da to ca phe den da saves 200+ cal. Still has the bold robusta flavor.
items.lean_version: Already zero cal. This IS the lean version.

items.dish_name: Canh Chua Ca — Caramelized Fish (Sour Fish Soup)
items.notes: Catfish or tilapia caramelized in clay pot with fish sauce, sugar, black pepper. Per Eamon's restaurant bible: the standout lean dish. High protein, moderate fat. The caramel sauce uses sugar but portion is small. Served without rice — add rice separately (~200 cal/cup).
items.lean_version: Already one of the leanest Vietnamese restaurant options. Skip the rice side or get half rice. 42g protein for 320 cal is excellent.

items.dish_name: Ca Kho To — Caramelized Fish in Clay Pot
items.notes: Catfish braised in caramel sauce (fish sauce + sugar + black pepper) in clay pot. Slightly more sauce/sugar than the sour fish version. Still extremely high protein. Usually served with steamed rice (not included in count). One of the best-kept secrets for lean Vietnamese eating.
items.lean_version: Pair with half rice and extra steamed vegetables. ~480 cal total meal with 40g+ protein.

items.dish_name: Bun Thit Nuong Cha Gio — Vermicelli + Grilled Pork + Fried Roll
items.notes: Combo bowl: vermicelli + grilled pork + 2 fried egg rolls + lettuce/herbs + nuoc cham. The most calorie-dense bun bowl because you get both grilled pork AND fried rolls. Popular combo plate at most Vietnamese restaurants.
items.lean_version: Get bun thit nuong (no cha gio) or swap fried rolls for fresh. Saves 200-300 cal.

items.dish_name: Ga Nuong — Grilled Lemongrass Chicken (Protein Only)
items.notes: Grilled lemongrass chicken — the protein component served on rice plates and vermicelli bowls. Usually chicken thigh. Marinade includes lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, oil. Good protein but thigh skin adds fat. Track this separately when building a custom bowl.
items.lean_version: Request skinless chicken breast. ~200 cal for same protein. Major fat savings.

items.notes: Grilled marinated pork — the protein on com tam, bun thit nuong, etc. Typically pork shoulder or loin marinated in fish sauce, sugar, garlic, lemongrass. The caramelized sugar crust adds flavor and a few grams of sugar. Fattier than chicken.
items.lean_version: Pork loin is leaner than shoulder. Ask for lean cut if available. Or swap to chicken. Saves ~60 cal.

items.notes: Bone-in pork chop, marinated and grilled. Standard protein on com tam plates. The bone adds no calories but reduces edible meat to ~140g. Marinade includes sugar and oil. Fat content varies widely based on cut thickness and trim.
items.lean_version: Trim visible fat before eating. Saves 50-80 cal. Or swap to chicken thigh/breast.

items.notes: Standard steamed jasmine rice served with Vietnamese entrees. Most restaurant plates serve 1.5-2 cups (~310-410 cal of just rice). This is the biggest calorie lever on any rice plate. USDA #20044.
items.lean_version: Half rice saves 100-200 cal per plate. Or ask for extra vegetables/salad instead of the full rice portion.

items.notes: Fractured jasmine rice grains — slightly different texture than regular rice but nearly identical calories. Used specifically in com tam dishes. Same calorie lever applies: the rice is usually the biggest calorie component of any plate.
items.lean_version: Half portion. Same strategy as regular rice.

items.notes: Thin round rice noodles (bun) used in vermicelli bowls. Slightly fewer calories than flat pho noodles. Very similar nutritionally to pho noodles — both are essentially pure starch. USDA rice vermicelli: 159 cal/100g cooked.
items.lean_version: Half noodles. Or request extra lettuce/herbs as the noodle bed instead.

items.notes: Sweet, thick soy-based sauce served alongside pho. Most people use 2-4 tbsp per bowl (80-160 cal). The sugar content is HIGH — 5g per tbsp. It's essentially liquid sugar with soy flavor. FatSecret: 40 cal/tbsp. Served at every pho restaurant in the squeeze bottle.
items.lean_version: Use sriracha instead (5 cal/tsp). Or use 1 tbsp max instead of flooding the bowl. Most people don't realize they're adding 100+ cal of pure sugar sauce.

items.notes: The rooster sauce — essentially free calories. 5 cal per tsp. Most people use 1-3 tsp. Even heavy usage is only 15 cal. Contains some sugar but negligible at normal portions. The BEST sauce choice at a Vietnamese restaurant for calorie consciousness.
items.lean_version: Already essentially zero cal. Use freely.

items.notes: Pure fish sauce — fermented anchovy extract. Almost zero calories but EXTREMELY high sodium (1,400-1,560mg per tbsp). CalorieKing: 50 cal/tbsp for A Taste Of Thai brand (that entry likely includes added sugar). Pure fish sauce is 6-10 cal. Great for flavor with minimal calorie cost. USDA fish sauce #06179.
items.lean_version: Already near zero calories. Watch sodium if tracking — 1 tbsp = 65% daily value.

items.notes: Fish sauce + sugar + lime juice + water + garlic + chili. The standard dipping sauce for spring rolls and the dressing on vermicelli bowls. More calories than straight fish sauce because of the added sugar. Most servings are 2-4 tbsp. Light enough to use freely.
items.lean_version: Ask for extra lime and less sugar in the mix. Or use straight fish sauce + lime squeeze. ~10 cal.

items.notes: Hoisin-based sauce with ground peanuts, served with fresh spring rolls. The peanut + hoisin combo packs sugar and fat in a small serving. San-J Thai peanut sauce: ~73 cal/tbsp. Vietnamese-style is slightly lighter. Most people use 2-4 tbsp per spring roll order (90-180 cal). Can DOUBLE the calories of fresh spring rolls.
items.lean_version: Use nuoc cham instead (25 cal/2 tbsp). Or dip lightly — 1 tbsp max. The spring rolls are lean; don't undo them with sauce.

items.dish_name: Pho Chay (Vegetable Pho — Broth + No Noodles)
items.notes: Vegetable broth with mushrooms, bok choy, tofu, bean sprouts — NO noodles. This is the absolute lowest calorie Vietnamese restaurant option. 80 cal for a full bowl. Add protein (chicken side: +140 cal) to make it a meal. Essentially a warm salad in broth form.
items.lean_version: Already the leanest possible order. Add protein to prevent hunger.
